speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number four. I'm speaking with George Eisenberg. And you just told me when they took you and your cousin by truck to Krosno.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russians. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were there for a short while. And then we went-- it was-- I remember it was just before Passover. From there, we went to Romania, to-- first to Hungary, to [NON-ENGLISH]. And then I was in Romania from '40-- '45-- I mean, from '40-- end '40-- '45 till, like, a half a year. I was in Romania, in a small town. We were doing pretty good in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do any work at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. From Romania, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and after the war, when the war end, in 1945, we started to-- I was there with my cousin. And my cousin was in Bucharest. But I was in a smaller town. So my cousin went from Bucharest, he was the first one, he went to Israel. And then I was in there, when I came from, I, I went-- we left from-- the town where I was was called [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a small town. We went to Vienna. When I--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you usually travel? Did you get on trains? Or walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I used to go train with no problem. I mean, first of all, I was using-- I was using a Russian uniform, so I could go on the military train. It was no problem. When I came to Vienna-- that's another piece of the-- my life. I started-- I-- we went to a-- in that time, I think it was the [NON-ENGLISH] Hospital. The [NON-ENGLISH] Hospital. But the people who came down there, I mean, there was already an organization which is called Berihah. And they came to the-- to the Banov Station, to the-- which-- to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Railroad station?

speaker: interviewee
text: Railroad station. And they picked me up. And they-- and, and they told me-- they took me into [NON-ENGLISH]. And they said to me that they know that I'm-- I have to stay with them. I'm not going to the camp. I have to work. I have to join the Berihah. They heard about me, that I am capable to do these things, I have to stay. I said, OK. I was not married in that time. I had nothing to lose.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain to me just what the Berihah was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Berihah means flight in Hebrew. I mean, it actually was what we called it-- flight and rescue. This was an organization which-- it started out like, we the-- were the one who started out the illegal immigration [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I came to Vienna after being there a few weeks, getting to know some people, so, we had to do some work. So, they gave me a mission. They told me I had to go to Lithuania to pick up a young kibbutz, people from a kibbutz to work, 26 people that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, the guy who was running the Berihah-- his name was [? Yitzak ?] [INAUDIBLE]. His wife was also one of them. So actually, what he wanted me to do, I should go ahead and bring back his wife plus these people. [INAUDIBLE] To tell you the story what have happened, I'm gonna give you some information. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They made-- we-- they gave me papers that I work for the International Red Cross. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I also had a-- I was going-- a friend of my-- I met a friend of mine. I mean, I got to know him. He was a Vienna-born Jew. So he gave me his passport. We falsified a passport that my name was [? Wilhelm ?] [INAUDIBLE]. And in that time, the transportation was very hard. I mean, the railroad connection was bad. But you could manage.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, I went on a train. And my first stop was, I came to, I mean, going from one station to the other. Finally, I wound up in Warsaw. In Warsaw, they gave me the address from Henrik-- what's it-- oh, I told you the name. Henrik Zuckerman was his name.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the leader from the Warsaw [INAUDIBLE]. Henrik Zuckerman. If I was-- so they gave me to go to him. I came in to him. And I told him that I have permission, I have to go to Vilna, that I have to bring out some people from there. He says to me, [? Jonalah, ?] don't you ever try to go any place. The border is closed. The Russians don't let nobody in and nobody out. He says, it's-- you're gonna get in a lot of trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, he says, I can't help you. I said, OK. He say, you need money? It's no problem. I'll give you the money you need. So I says, what good here am I? I say, OK. So, the only thing I could do, we have some connection in Bialystok. Bialystok is near the border from Lithuania. So, he says, go in. You go into this and this person-- forgot her name-- this woman. And see what you can do. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to Bialystok. I come in in there. And I tell her the whole story. She says, well, I'm sorry. Nothing we can do for you. OK. In the meantime, there was going on, in-- the Berihah was already-- it was going, it was popular already. People knew already, the Jewish people knew, that they're going, there's going to be a ghetto and they're going from one place to the other, and eventually, they're going to wind up in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, people were-- it was called, you know, the black market. And Russia's always a black market. There were-- people used to meet. So finally, I got in touch with one guy. I got-- became friendly with a Russian Jewish fellow. He was a sergeant. And I became friendly with him. And finally, he-- well, he told-- he told me once, he says, listen. He had trust in me. He said, listen, you know something? I heard there's going on-- there's something going on, that people are moving to Austria to Germany, and from there they go to Israel. He says, I would like to be one of them. So, in the beginning, I was afraid, you know? But finally, as we got friendlier, so I came up to him. And I says, you know what I could do? I could make a deal with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, you're a Russian soldier. You're a sergeant. I says, suppose you give me your papers and I'm the one, I'm going to send you, I'll give you connections. I'm going to send you to Lodz. From Lodz, they're going to go to Czechoslovakia, to Bratislava. And from there, you'll go to Salzburg and you'll be OK. He says, OK. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember this man's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I forgot it. [INAUDIBLE] I forgot his name. I know his first name was Israel. But I don't know his second name for [INAUDIBLE] so many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, he says, OK. So how are we going to work this, he says. The guy was a tall guy. I'm a little not. How the hell am I going to fit in this uniform? But in Russia, you could do it. You cut a little bit in here, you make it a little smaller there. It's OK. So, to show you how dumb I was in that time-- so, he says to me, he says, you know what? I'm going to teach you all what you have to know about it. And you have to be, like-- tomorrow night, it was on a Saturday night, he said the best time to travel. Because you had the, the [NON-ENGLISH], which is called the, the permit. Like a furlough permit. Right? That you have to be back tomorrow. Otherwise, you're gonna be court-martialed. So, he says, it'll work out fine for you. So, OK. So, he teaches me all these things. I'm putting on his uniform. And I have all his papers, all stamped, everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: I go into the train. To military. You gotta go in the military train. In the military. My Russian was good in that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, on a Saturday night, maybe 2:00, 3 o'clock in the morning, the train pulls in and-- to Grodno. And that was the checkpoint, in Grodno. The train pulls in, like, to a tunnel. There's only one entrance. Only one person could go. That's how now.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, in the morning, we-- it's already-- it's also on a summer day. And we coming out. And so, all of a sudden, the train stops. And, you know, I'm a little guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, the military trains in that time, they didn't have no seat. They just had bunks, you know, like three-stage bunk. So I climbed on the top of the bunks. I wanted to be sure. You know. So I'm sitting in-- laying in there. And the guys, the Russian come in. And they started hollering, everybody out, everybody out, everybody out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Checkpoint. Checkpoint. I make believe I don't know from nothing. I sleeping.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally, a soldier comes in. He wakes me up. He says, hey, listen. You gotta go down to the checkpoint. I say, what. What checkpoint? Yeah, yeah, he says. [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], the Russian. OK. Coming down the checkpoint, so you go down. And then you have to climb up, maybe three, four stories. It was like a-- it was a big hill, you know? And only one person could go up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we're coming into the barrack and the checkpoint. A Russian lieutenant is sitting in there. I'm coming up in front of him. He says to me, let me see your papers. Show him the papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: He looking at me. He say, you Sergeant so and so? I say, yeah. What are you doing in here? I-- what do you mean, what am I doing. I'm going back to my-- to my unit. Where's your unit? I said, Druskininkai. I still remember that name. [INAUDIBLE] yeah, OK. He say, you know something? You can't go in there. I say, wait a minute. What do you mean I can't go?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, because you don't have the stamp-- you, in Russian, you've got to have-- the more stamps you have, then better you are. Then you-- a better citizen you are. He say, you don't have-- you're missing one stamp from the headquarters from Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: I say, what are you talking about? I have all the stamps. He said, listen. Don't argue with me. He said, that's what it is. He say, you're going back to Warsaw. I said, no. I'm not going back to Warsaw. He say, OK. You're not going back?

speaker: interviewee
text: The soldier, he says, [INAUDIBLE], take him away. Lock him up. OK. They're bring me in and they're locking me up. Ought to give you an idea of how stupid I was. I'm wearing a Russian uniform. I'm a Russian sergeant. Underneath, I'm wearing civilian clothes. I have papers that I work International Red Cross. I have a paper that I am working-- my name is [? Wilhelm ?] [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: I have German money. I have dollars. And I have a list that I have to take out 26 people from Vilna. OK? So I'm looking around. I say, how the hell and-- how am I going to get away from here? That's-- things looks bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lucky I was. I was with the Russian army in there. I didn't have to take my clothes off to sleep. We were sleeping in bunkers. Because this was the border between-- between [? Lithua ?] and Poland. So there-- we had the bunkers underneath the hills. So I didn't take off no. On the third day, I says, well, that's the end of me. The third day, these two soldiers who bought me in there the first time, they come back to me. They're coming back. They say, listen. You gotta go back to headquarters, to the checkpoint. OK. They bring me back to the same guy. And the guy says to me, he said, listen. He says, there's two ways. He says, either I'm going to lock you up or send you to Siberia, or if not, you're going back to Warsaw to get that stamp. I said, well, one thing-- on that time, I didn't lose my head. I said to him, I said, listen. I'll tell you what. Under one condition I'm going back. You give me a paper stating that you kept me here for three days. Because if I come back to my unit, they're going to prosecute me. They're gonna court-martial me. Because I'm not there. I was supposed to be on Sunday. Today's already Wednesday.

speaker: interviewer
text: He say, oh, yeah. That's no problem. OK. I says, yeah, no problem? I says, you give me all the information where I have to go to Warsaw and I'll see you next week. OK. So he gives me a paper that he detained me there for three days. And he gives me some canned food. And he gives me some bread. And he's telling the guys to take me up to the train, go back to Warsaw. So they put me up on the train and I came back to Warsaw. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I came back to Warsaw, I came back to Henrik Zuckerman, and I tell him the story, he says to me, in Jewish, you know something? [SPEAKING HEBREW], you have more luck than your brains are worth. Now, that's one story. And then, of course, I made sure-- so being I had all these papers, so I came to Lodz. I took a week, or two weeks, I took a-- like a little vacation there. Had a good time. And I came back to Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: They said, listen. [? Jonalah, ?] why should you waste your papers? He say, you have papers International Red Cross. They said, why don't you take another 26 people back to Bratislava?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, OK. No problem. So I took another 26 people back to Bratislava. And I left them in there in Bratislava. And on that time, I was in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's how I came to Vienna. Vienna, we were there, in a [NON-ENGLISH] 2. That was our headquarters from Berihah. From then-- OK, you want to go on? Oh, sure. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: From Vienna, I was a couple of months, a few months in Vienna. Then, they-- the Berihah decided that we have to open a new border, we have to establish a new border, between Austria and Germany. And you the one, and two other guys, you have to go in, make connections, from Salzburg. Because Salzburg is near the border between Ainring and Bad Reichenhall, you know. And, so, so, you've gotta make connection.

speaker: interviewer
text: So sure enough, we went in there. We were like the-- the three of us, we were like the first reconnaissance group. So, sure enough, we got caught the first night.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to the border. We got caught by the Austrian police. Came back [INAUDIBLE] the Austrian police. Of course, in Salzburg, already, was a Jewish camp in there. And we had the, the headquarters from Berihah, was [NON-ENGLISH] Strasse 25. And the camp was Camp [? Reidenburg. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So, when I got caught by the Austrians, told them, I said, listen, we're trying-- we're trying to cross the border because we have family I have to-- want to go there to visit. Bad Reichenhall. There's another Jewish camp in there. So, blah, blah, blah, this, this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we took these two guys, and, and we said to them, we said, listen. Here's some food. In that time, food was scarce. So we gave him-- we had food. We gave him some food. We gave him some cigarettes. Was a big [INAUDIBLE]. And we became friendly with them. So we made out with them guys that we became so friendly, that they were looking the other way. Whenever we had to bring some people over the border, we knew exactly where they are and when they are. And we used to start smuggling these people from one side to the other. And this is how I started with the Berihah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the refugees--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that you smuggled, it wasn't that all of them wanted to go to Israel. It was rather that they wanted to leave their country of origin? And go other places?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, most of them, they were under the assumption that-- I mean, we, the Berihah, was under the assumption that they gonna wind up in Israel, which eventually, they did. But also, there was-- well, if I start telling these stories, it'll be till tomorrow. Forget about it. We also have work from, from Vienna to, to [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] was the English, the British zone in there. And the British were not too eager to help the Jews. Because they were against the whole-- the whole [INAUDIBLE]. They were against it. Matter of fact, I got caught once by the British in [NON-ENGLISH]. And they brought me in front of a general. And the guy says to me, he says, listen. I know-- I know all about it, that you guys are smuggling people. And you dump 'em in our-- in the British soil. He says, I give you a warning. He say, if I catch you again, he says, I'm going to keep you in here. You'll never go back. I say, OK. All right. I still went back. He never caught me. He never-- it's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can you tell me about the American officer Novinsky?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, Captain Novinsky, he was very-- he was the number one man. He used to help us a lot. As a matter of fact, he was-- they made in-- a few years ago, they honored him, the Jew-- the Israeli government honor him as the most-- what do you call it? The most valuable or the most heroic person for the-- what he did for the Jewish Berihah. He was the one who, he used to give us [NON-ENGLISH] documents, so we could-- [INAUDIBLE]. About Captain Novinsky, I gotta tell you this story. One time, we were sending people-- also, we were sending people to all different zones. Germany and Austria were divided between three governments, or three people. It was between the American zone, was the British zone, and it was the Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what we used to do, the first thing, was the main job was to bring the people out from the Russian zone. To bring them from the Russian zone, we used to bring them to the American zone. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: One time, also, in there, [INAUDIBLE] we were-- I was bringing people over from Vienna, to the American zone. To [INAUDIBLE]. That was the border in there. Linz, near-- we had a camp in Linz. And for some reasons, the, the Americans didn't want to let any people in there. They didn't want to let in any Jews from the Russian zone to the American.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was in the train. And, and they came on the train. And they were asking, who is the leader? Who brings you in here?

speaker: interviewee
text: One stupid girl said-- points on me. She say, he's the one. OK. So, they took me off the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the soldier was about 6 foot tall. And he says to me, why do you do this? I said, what am I doing? I don't do nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: He say, they told me that you're the leader. But I say, I'm a nobody. I says, I'm the same one, like, like any other one. He say, no, that's not true. He smacked me over my face that I could still remember. [LAUGHS] That-- but still [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened? They still took off the transport with me together. And they put us in in a camp. It was called Wels.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wels, [INAUDIBLE], in this place where they kept us, it was the same place where they kept the Nazis, the, the, the people who, from the German army, who escaped. Who-- I mean, who not escape. Who were--

speaker: interviewer
text: War criminals.

speaker: interviewee
text: --taken-- war criminals. We the one-- between the war criminals. But later on, we had connections in there, in Linz. They came in after a few days and they took us out from there. Then-- going, going further now. So this was going from Russian zone to the American. Then, we used to bring people from-- to-- from Salzburg to Innsbruck. This is after I was sent from Vienna to Salzburg, I was stationed in Salzburg. So my job was going, bringing people to the French zone, which was in Innsbruck. And to the German zone, to the American zone in Germany, which was by the Reichenhall, Ainring, Bad Reichenhall.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, one time, we were-- of course, in that time, we already-- we were working together with the Israeli brigade. There was the British brigade, there was a Jewish brigade in the British army. And they the ones who were-- well, I mean, they were the soldiers. They were government, legal. So we work together with them. So, one time, we had to bring some people to Innsbruck. So, showing up, what did they do? They sent-- [NON-ENGLISH], you have to go to Innsbruck. So I say, OK. No problem. OK. No problem. So, I'm dressed in an American uniform, as a soldier. This is 12 o'clock at midnight. On top of my uniform-- it was a rainy day-- I'm wearing a civilian raincoat. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm having with me, papers, passports. We had to bring some Israelis from the government, we had to bring them to Salzburg. So we made false passports for them. So I had about seven passports in my pocket. I had the list of the people who I'm taking. I had American dollars with me, which was illegal. I had German Reichsmark, which was illegal. And I had Austrian schillings, which was legal. Two MPs come over to me. And they said, papers. Because my English, even it's bad now. But it wasn't-- it was much worse. I didn't know much English. I knew a couple of words in there. [INAUDIBLE], papers, papers. Show them papers. Guy says, come on. Take you away. They put me in a Jeep and they bring me to the headquarters, to the American headquarters, army headquarters. OK. Bring me to American headquarters, they start searching me. They find all these goodies what I have. And they say, well, we gotta put you to jail. OK. Put me in jail. Put me in jail. I was there for a couple of days. In the meantime, nobody knew where I disappeared. This friend, this-- my boss, I mean, he was a friend and a boss. His name was [NON-ENGLISH]. He was in charge.

speaker: interviewer
text: They call in Innsbruck. [? Jonalah ?] didn't arrive in there. So they start worrying. They say, what happened to him? Nobody knows.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't communicate with them, with nobody. So finally, after a few days, I became friendly with the prison guard. You know, I had cigarettes I had with me. In that time, cigarettes was a great thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I smoke a cigarette. I start smoking. And the guy says to me, can I have a cigarette? I say, yeah, sure you can have a cigarette. I say, I tell you what. You want to have more cigarettes? He say, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, I tell you what. Here's the name. You go down to [NON-ENGLISH] Strasse 25 and see [NON-ENGLISH]. And tell him that [? Jonalah ?] is sitting in jail. OK. He say, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have to interrupt now, for a tape change.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
